name: Puerto Lápice – circular desde Consuegra
region_intro: Mountain biking around Consuegra offers diverse terrain, blending the vast plains of La Mancha with the rolling hills of Cerro Calderico. The region features varied landscapes, from steppe-like areas to isolated hills, and is in proximity to the more rugged Montes de Toledo. These geographical features provide a range of unpaved segments and elevation changes suitable for mountain bike trails. The area's unique composition includes historic windmills and a medieval castle atop a ridge, creating scenic and challenging routes. Best mountain bike trails around Consuegra The most popular mountain bike trail is Puerto Lápice – circular desde Consuegra, a 31.9 miles (51.3 km) trail that takes 4 hours 15 minutes to complete, traversing the open plains and offering expansive views. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Consuegra Windmills – Fuente del Umbrión loop from Consuegra, a moderate 30.8 miles (49.6 km) path. This route incorporates the iconic windmills and varied rural scenery. Local mountain bikers also love the Puerto Lápice Windmills – Fuente del Umbrión loop from Consuegra, a 39.3 miles (63.2 km) trail leading through the plains and towards the windmills, often completed in about 5 hours 12 minutes. Mountain biking around Consuegra is defined by historic windmills, expansive plains, and isolated hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides across the countryside to more difficult routes with significant elevation gain. The routes in Consuegra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 400 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Consuegra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available around Consuegra?
A: There are over 15 mountain bike trails around Consuegra, offering a range of experiences from moderate rides across the countryside to more difficult routes with significant elevation gain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on mountain bike trails in Consuegra?
A: Mountain biking in Consuegra features diverse terrain, blending the vast plains of La Mancha with the rolling hills of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3902682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Calderico&lt;/a&gt;. You'll encounter steppe-like areas, isolated hills, and unpaved segments, with some routes venturing towards the more rugged Montes de Toledo.

Q: Are there any challenging mountain bike routes in Consuegra for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Consuegra offers routes suitable for experienced riders. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31867812" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Lápice Windmills – Fuente del Umbrión loop from Consuegra&lt;/a&gt; is rated as difficult, covering 39.3 miles (63.2 km) and often taking over 5 hours to complete, with notable elevation changes.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see while mountain biking in Consuegra?
A: Many routes incorporate the region's iconic historical landmarks. You can cycle past the famous windmills atop &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3902682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Calderico&lt;/a&gt; and the medieval &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2654903" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Consuegra Castle&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20133742" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Consuegra Castle – Calderico Hill loop from Consuegra&lt;/a&gt; is a great option to explore these sites.

Q: Are there any family-friendly cycling routes in Consuegra?
A: While many mountain bike trails in Consuegra are moderate to difficult, some sections of the plains might be suitable for families with older children or those on robust touring bikes. Always check the route's difficulty and elevation profile on komoot before heading out.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in Consuegra?
A: The mountain bike trails in Consuegra are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from more than 10 reviews. Riders often praise the scenic views, especially around the windmills, and the varied terrain that provides a good challenge.

Q: Can I find circular mountain bike routes in Consuegra?
A: Yes, many mountain bike routes in Consuegra are designed as loops. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022671559/puerto-lapice-circular-desde-consuegra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Lápice – circular desde Consuegra&lt;/a&gt;, a 31.9-mile (51.3 km) trail that traverses open plains and offers expansive views.

Q: Are there any routes that specifically feature the windmills?
A: Absolutely. The iconic windmills are a central feature of many routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20133888" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Consuegra Windmills – Fuente del Umbrión loop from Consuegra&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate path that incorporates these historic structures and varied rural scenery.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in Consuegra?
A: The best time for mountain biking in Consuegra is typically during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, and while winters are generally mild, some areas might experience seasonal rain, affecting trail conditions.

Q: Are there any natural features or wildlife to look out for on the trails?
A: Beyond the historical sites, the region offers diverse natural landscapes. You'll encounter steppe-like plains and isolated hills. The proximity to the Montes de Toledo means there's potential for spotting wildlife like deer, wild boar, vultures, and eagles in more rugged areas, especially near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3902682" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calderico Hill&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any longer mountain bike routes for endurance riders?
A: For endurance riders, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31867812" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto Lápice Windmills – Fuente del Umbrión loop from Consuegra&lt;/a&gt; offer a substantial challenge, covering nearly 40 miles (63.2 km) with significant elevation gain, making for a rewarding long-distance ride.

Q: What are some other interesting places to explore near the mountain bike trails?
A: Beyond the main trails, you can explore other nearby settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2654920" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Yébenes&lt;/a&gt; or visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5455439" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of Valdehierro&lt;/a&gt;, which can be integrated into longer rides or visited separately.
tips: One of the most picturesque and entertaining routes in the interior of Toledo, visiting the castle and the windmills of Consuegra while allowing you to appreciate and enjoy a beautiful landscape with very beautiful views from the crest of Cerro Calderico.

In 1925 this mill was still in operation. The neighbours could access it to grind wheat, either via the horse trail located on its eastern side, or via the road called La Nava for carts. This mill also had a nearby building, the miller's hut, and a dividing wall was also built to allow livestock to be kept.

Las Cabezuelas is an old Roman silver mine located on the outskirts of Camuñas (Toledo). A large cross and a tombstone give some clues to its gloomy use in the first months of the last Civil War: a mass grave for priests, workers, owners, industrialists and farmers suspected of sympathizing with the Francoist side. Coinciding with the fury of excavators that has led to the Law of Historical Memory in mass graves but without claiming any subsidy, the Archbishopric of Toledo has financed an exhaustive analysis of this mine. The result: the location of 41 people, mostly men. However, all the hypotheses point out that at least 100 more people are buried in the depths. All of them, like those persecuted by the national side that still remain anonymous in so many ditches, victims of the same hatred.
The intention of the Church has not been to stir up old stories of rancor and murder, but rather to identify a group of priests who ended their days in this mine and on whom the process of their beatification began in 2002. Their location has been possible because their names were inventoried in the General Cause that General Franco opened in 1939 to honor his memory.
More information: https://www.larazon.es/memoria-e-historia/20210128/cqepqq5qy5g5zhjodi73qkuipa.html?outputType=amp
